content:
Hi. I recently need to write a driver capable of creating some chrdevs. I find I need to add all the registration items to the struct implementing kernel::Module trait if I want to see these chrdevs in the /proc/devices. The name of the registration items has no influence, but the content matters. It needs to be Pin<Box<chrdev::Registration<N>>>.

The struct should be

struct RustChrdev {
    _dev: Pin<Box<chrdev::Registration<N>>>,
}

rather than

struct RustChrdev;

I read the source code of the macro in the function module. The driver struct is matched as __MOD as follows.

fn __init() -> kernel::c_types::c_int {{
    match <{type_} as kernel::Module>::init(kernel::c_str!(\"{name}\"), &THIS_MODULE) {{
        Ok(m) => {{
            unsafe {{
                __MOD = Some(m);
            }}
            return 0;
        }}
        Err(e) => {{
            return e.to_kernel_errno();
        }}
    }}
}}

I don't find any other routines about __MOD instead of drop. I don't understand why we need to put the Registration in the driver struct. The alloc_chrdev_region and cdev_add are already called, so the chrdev should be created. Thanks for your help!
__exit will drop __MOD, which in turn recursively drops things. chrdev::Registration has a Drop implementation which de-registers the device.

I notice the __MOD is dropped in the __exit() function. What I don't understand is why the rust_chrdev does not show up in the /proc/devices file if we don't put the chrdev::Registration in the struct.

I use the sample/rust_chrdev.rs to do an experiment. I comment the chrdev::Registration in the RustChrdev and return OK(). Then, the rust_chrdev can not be seen from the /proc/devices file. I originally think this will only influence the drop part. Because the alloc_chrdev_region and cdev_add are already called. So the rust_chrdev should show up in the /proc/devices file. But the result is far from what I expected. I don't have a clue about the reason.

struct RustChrdev {
    // _dev: Pin<Box<chrdev::Registration<2>>>, The first commented line
}

impl kernel::Module for RustChrdev {
    fn init(name: &'static CStr, module: &'static ThisModule) -> Result<Self> {
        pr_info!("Rust character device sample (init)\n");

        let mut chrdev_reg: Pin<Box<chrdev::Registration<2>>> = chrdev::Registration::new_pinned(name, 0, module)?;

        // Register the same kind of device twice, we're just demonstrating
        // that you can use multiple minors. There are two minors in this case
        // because its type is `chrdev::Registration<2>`
        chrdev_reg.as_mut().register::<RustFile>()?;
        chrdev_reg.as_mut().register::<RustFile>()?;

        Ok(RustChrdev {}) // The added line
        // Ok(RustChrdev { _dev: chrdev_reg }) The second commented line
    }
}
If you don't put it in the struct then chrdev_reg is dropped as soon as init returns, and dropping that struct deregisters the value.
Thank you both very much! I'm bothered by this for a long while. I did not consider how long will chrdev_reg live.
Hongyu Li has marked this topic as resolved.
I have some questions further upon Pin<Box<T>>. Why must Registration be pinned? Could it just be on the stack without allocation?

The Registration has a member of the C struct cdev, with the list-head inside. Therefore the struct Registration is self-referential. This requires Registration to be pinned. Wedson explained in detail why using Pin in this video.
@Hongyu Li Thanks! The video just explained all I need!
